summary: Paul Graham argues that startup funding has shifted from a two-player world (angels and VCs) to a three-player market with super-angels, creating real competition and changing deal structure. This competition is raising valuations, speeding rounds, and giving founders more leverage, though it also pressures VCs and introduces new dynamics like signaling risk.
key_points: Super-angels emerged to fill the mid-sized funding gap and now compete with both angels and VCs.
Competition between super-angels and VCs drives faster rounds, higher valuations, and less dilution for founders.
VCs are threatened but may retain advantages in brand and hands-on support for select winners.
Medium-sized angel rounds let founders preserve control and reduce fundraising stress compared to traditional Series A.
Signaling risk from VC participation exists but is often overstated if a startup shows strong results.
money_quotes: After barely changing at all for decades, the startup funding business is now in what could, at least by comparison, be called turmoil.
The arrival of a new type of investor is big news for startups, because there used to be only two and they rarely competed with one another.
Till now, VCs' claims about how much value they added were sort of like the government's.
Rate of return is what matters in investing—not the multiple you get, but the multiple per year.
As a rule, the only thing that can kill a good startup is the startup itself.
key_insight: The rise of super-angels turns startup funding into a competitive market that improves terms for founders while reshaping how and why VCs invest.
